title: Share of adults who are obese vs. daily protein supply
subtitle: Obesity is defined as having a body-mass index (BMI) equal to, or greater than, 30. BMI is a person's weight (in kilograms) divided by their height (in meters) squared.
caption:
Share of adults who are obese vs. daily protein supply

Obesity is defined as having a body-mass index (BMI) equal to, or greater than, 30. BMI is a person's weight (in kilograms) divided by their height (in meters) squared.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between daily protein supply in grams per day per capita on the horizontal axis and the share of adults who are obese on the vertical axis for the year 2022. The data points are color-coded by region, with North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania represented by distinct hues. A general positive trend is visible, where countries with higher protein supplies tend to have higher obesity rates, though significant variation exists. At the lower end of the protein scale, between 40 and 60 grams, countries like Burundi, Somalia, and Bangladesh cluster with obesity rates below 15%. In the middle range, around 80 to 100 grams, nations such as Colombia, Algeria, and the World average sit between 15% and 30% obesity. The upper right quadrant features countries with protein supplies exceeding 120 grams, including the United States, Ireland, and Iceland, where obesity rates range from roughly 23% to over 30%. Several Pacific Island nations, including Nauru, Tonga, and Samoa, appear as outliers with obesity rates exceeding 60% despite varying protein supply levels. Conversely, China and Vietnam show lower obesity rates, under 10%, despite protein supplies near or above 100 grams. The chart illustrates a broad correlation where increased protein availability often coincides with higher prevalence of obesity across the global dataset.
